questions.prompt: Which of the following operations require an `unsafe` block (or an `unsafe fn`)? Select all that apply.

options.A: Dereferencing a raw pointer such as `*const i32`
options.B: Calling an `unsafe` function or method
options.C: Creating a raw pointer from a reference with `&raw const num`
options.D: Reading or modifying a mutable static variable (`static mut`)

questions.explanation: The five unsafe superpowers are: dereference raw pointers, call unsafe functions, access or modify mutable statics, implement unsafe traits, and access union fields. Creating a raw pointer (C) is perfectly safe -- only dereferencing it requires `unsafe`.

questions.prompt: A colleague claims: "Inside an `unsafe` block, Rust turns off the borrow checker, so I can create two mutable references to the same value." What is true?

options.A: Correct -- `unsafe` disables all compiler safety checks inside the block
options.B: Incorrect -- `unsafe` does not turn off the borrow checker; references are still fully checked, and only the five extra operations become available
options.C: Correct, but only for references to local variables, not to heap data
options.D: Incorrect -- `unsafe` disables the borrow checker only for `'static` references

questions.explanation: `unsafe` does not disable the borrow checker or any other safety checks: if you use a reference in unsafe code, it is still checked. The keyword only grants access to the five superpowers that the compiler cannot verify for memory safety. To alias mutable data you would need raw pointers, not references.

questions.prompt: What is the result of compiling and running this program?

options.A: Prints `5`
options.B: Prints `10`
options.C: Compile error: cannot have a `*const` and a `*mut` pointer to `num` at the same time
options.D: Compiles, but dereferencing `r1` is undefined behavior because `r2` exists

questions.explanation: Creating both raw pointers is safe: raw pointers are exempt from the borrow-checking rules that forbid a simultaneous immutable and mutable borrow. Inside the `unsafe` block, `*r2 = 10` writes through the mutable raw pointer, then `*r1` reads the same memory, printing `10`. Both pointers derive from a valid local, so this is well defined.

questions.prompt: Why does this program fail to compile?

options.A: `unsafe fn` may only be defined inside an `unsafe` module
options.B: An `unsafe` function must take at least one raw-pointer parameter
options.C: Calling an `unsafe` function must happen inside an `unsafe` block (or another unsafe context), so `dangerous()` in `main` is rejected
options.D: The body of `dangerous` is empty, and empty unsafe functions are forbidden

questions.explanation: Calling an unsafe function is one of the five superpowers, so it requires an unsafe context: `unsafe { dangerous(); }`. By calling it inside `unsafe`, you assert that you have read the function's contract and are upholding its safety requirements.

questions.prompt: The standard library's `split_at_mut` returns two mutable slices into one slice. Its implementation uses raw pointers and `slice::from_raw_parts_mut` inside `unsafe`, yet the function itself is safe to call. Why is this a valid "safe abstraction"?

options.A: The borrow checker cannot see that the two returned slices never overlap; the `assert!(mid <= len)` plus the pointer arithmetic guarantee both slices are in bounds and disjoint, so no caller can trigger undefined behavior
options.B: Marking a function safe automatically makes any `unsafe` code inside it sound, as long as it compiles
options.C: `slice::from_raw_parts_mut` performs its own runtime bounds checking, so the `unsafe` block is just a formality
options.D: It is not actually valid -- a purely safe implementation with two indexing expressions would also compile and should be preferred

questions.explanation: A purely safe implementation fails to compile (E0499) because the borrow checker only sees two mutable borrows of the same slice; it cannot understand that `[..mid]` and `[mid..]` are disjoint. The unsafe implementation is sound because `assert!(mid <= len)` guarantees the raw pointers stay in bounds and the two ranges cannot overlap. Wrapping verified unsafe code in a safe API is exactly how safe abstractions work.

questions.prompt: Consider this foreign function declaration. Which statement is true?

options.A: `"C"` names the application binary interface (ABI) to use, and calling `abs` requires an unsafe context because other languages do not enforce Rust's safety guarantees
options.B: `"C"` means the function's source code must be written in C89; C++ functions need `extern "C++"` and are safe to call
options.C: Functions declared in `extern` blocks are safe to call because the linker verifies their signatures at build time
options.D: `extern` blocks can only import functions; Rust functions can never be exported for C code to call

questions.explanation: `extern "C"` declares functions using the C ABI. Calling them is unsafe because foreign code does not uphold Rust's guarantees and the compiler cannot check it. The reverse direction also exists: `#[no_mangle] pub extern "C" fn ...` exports a Rust function so C code can call it, so D is false.

questions.prompt: `Iterator` is defined with an associated type (`type Item;`) instead of a generic parameter (`trait Iterator<T>`). What key difference motivates that choice?

options.A: Associated types allow one type to implement the trait many times with different `Item` types
options.B: With an associated type, a type can implement `Iterator` only once, fixing `Item` in that single impl, so callers never need annotations to disambiguate which implementation to use
options.C: Generic parameters on traits are deprecated and will be removed in a future edition
options.D: Associated types are resolved at runtime via vtables, while generic parameters are resolved at compile time

questions.explanation: With `trait Iterator<T>`, `Counter` could implement both `Iterator<u32>` and `Iterator<String>`, forcing callers to annotate which one they mean on every `next` call. An associated type permits only one `impl Iterator for Counter`, which fixes `type Item = u32` once, so the item type is unambiguous. A is backwards, and both mechanisms are compile-time constructs.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: Compile error: `Add` can only be implemented with `Rhs = Self`

questions.explanation: `Add` is declared as `trait Add<Rhs = Self>`, so `Self` is only the default -- overriding it with `impl Add<Meters> for Millimeters` is exactly the customization the default type parameter enables. The impl converts meters to millimeters: `500 + 2 * 1000 = 2500`.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `waving waving waving `
options.B: `captain up waving `
options.C: Compile error: `Human` cannot define `fly` when two traits already provide it
options.D: `captain up ` followed by a compile error on `person.fly()` because the call is ambiguous

questions.explanation: Because `fly` takes `self`, `Trait::fly(&person)` disambiguates by trait, printing `captain ` then `up `. A plain method call `person.fly()` prefers the inherent impl over trait methods, printing `waving `. All three coexist legally; nothing is ambiguous here.

questions.prompt: This program fails to compile at the `println!` line. What is wrong, and what is the fix?

options.A: `baby_name` has no `self` parameter, so Rust cannot tell which implementor of `Animal` is meant; use fully qualified syntax: `<Dog as Animal>::baby_name()`
options.B: A type may not have both an inherent function and a trait function with the same name; rename one of them
options.C: Associated functions in traits cannot return `String`; return `&'static str` instead
options.D: `Animal::baby_name()` works, but only after adding `use Animal;` inside `main`

questions.explanation: Associated functions without a `self` receiver give the compiler no way to infer the implementing type -- other types could also implement `Animal`. Fully qualified syntax `<Dog as Animal>::baby_name()` names both the type and the trait, and prints `puppy`. (`Dog::baby_name()` would call the inherent function and print `Spot`.)

questions.prompt: Why does this code fail to compile?

options.A: A trait cannot have a default method body that calls `to_string`
options.B: `OutlinePrint` declares `fmt::Display` as a supertrait, but `Point` does not implement `Display`, so the `impl OutlinePrint for Point` is rejected
options.C: `impl OutlinePrint for Point {}` is empty, and empty impl blocks are not allowed
options.D: Supertrait syntax requires `where Self: fmt::Display` instead of a colon

questions.explanation: `trait OutlinePrint: fmt::Display` means every implementor must also implement `Display`; that supertrait bound is what lets the default body call `self.to_string()`. Since `Point` lacks a `Display` impl, the compiler reports that the trait bound `Point: std::fmt::Display` is not satisfied. Adding `impl fmt::Display for Point` fixes it. Empty impl blocks are fine, and the colon syntax is correct.

questions.prompt: You want `Display` output for `Vec<String>`, but both `Display` and `Vec<String>` are defined outside your crate, so the orphan rule blocks `impl Display for Vec<String>`. How does the newtype pattern solve this?

options.A: `Wrapper` is a local type, so implementing the external trait `Display` on it satisfies the orphan rule; the inner vector is reached via `self.0`, and the wrapper adds no runtime overhead
options.B: The newtype tells the compiler to treat `Vec<String>` as if it were declared in the current crate, so the orphan rule no longer applies to `Vec<String>` itself
options.C: It works, but each access to the inner `Vec` goes through an extra heap indirection at runtime
options.D: `Wrapper` automatically inherits every method of `Vec<String>`, including `push` and `len`

questions.explanation: The orphan rule requires the trait or the type to be local; `Wrapper` is a local tuple struct, so the impl is allowed. Newtypes are zero-cost -- the wrapper is elided at compile time. The downside is that `Wrapper` does NOT automatically expose `Vec`'s methods (D is false); you must delegate manually or implement `Deref`.

questions.prompt: Given `type Kilometers = i32;`, a developer assumes the compiler will now reject `fn add_miles(m: i32)` being called with a `Kilometers` value, catching unit mix-ups. What is true?

options.A: Correct -- a type alias creates a distinct new type, exactly like a tuple struct
options.B: Incorrect -- a type alias is only a synonym: `Kilometers` and `i32` are the same type and fully interchangeable, so no unit confusion is caught; a newtype like `struct Kilometers(i32)` would be needed for that
options.C: Correct, but only when the alias is declared `pub`
options.D: Incorrect -- aliases do create new types, but the coercion rules silently convert between them

questions.explanation: `type` creates a synonym, not a new type. Values of type `Kilometers` are treated identically to `i32` everywhere, so mixing them passes type checking. Aliases exist to reduce repetition of long types (e.g. `type Thunk = Box<dyn Fn() + Send + 'static>`), not to add type safety. For safety you want the newtype pattern.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.B: Compile error: cannot add `i32` to `Kilometers`
options.C: Compile error: type aliases may not be declared inside a function

questions.explanation: Because `Kilometers` is an alias, `y` really has type `i32`, so `x + y` is ordinary `i32` addition and prints `x + y = 10`. Type aliases can be declared inside functions.

questions.prompt: Which statements about the never type `!` and dynamically sized types (DSTs) are true? Select all that apply.

options.A: An expression of type `!` can be coerced into any other type, which is why one `match` arm can be `continue` while another produces a `u32`
options.B: `continue`, `panic!`, and a `loop` with no `break` all have type `!`
options.C: `str` (the type itself, not `&str`) has a size known at compile time
options.D: `&str` is a "fat pointer": it stores the address of the string data plus its length
options.E: Writing `fn generic<T: ?Sized>(t: &T)` allows `T` to be a DST, but the parameter must then sit behind some pointer type such as `&T`

questions.explanation: `!` has no values, so it can never actually produce one -- the compiler lets it coerce into whatever type the context demands, which is why `Err(_) => continue` type-checks alongside `Ok(num) => num`. `str` alone is a DST (its length varies per value), so C is false: you must use it behind a pointer like `&str` or `Box<str>`. `&str` stores address + length, and `?Sized` relaxes the implicit `Sized` bound while requiring indirection.

questions.prompt: Why does this code fail to compile?

options.A: String literals have type `String`, so the annotation should be `String`
options.B: `str` is a dynamically sized type: its size is not known at compile time, so a bare local variable cannot have type `str`; use `&str`, which is a fat pointer with a known size
options.C: Two `str` variables cannot exist in the same scope because they would alias the same string table
options.D: The code compiles; `str` and `&str` are interchangeable

questions.explanation: Every value of a given type must occupy the same amount of memory, but `"Hello there!"` and "How's it going?" would need 12 and 15 bytes respectively, so `str` cannot be stored directly. The golden rule for DSTs: always put them behind a pointer -- `&str`, `Box<str>`, `Rc<str>`, etc. String literals have type `&'static str`, not `String`.

questions.prompt: Given `fn do_twice(f: fn(i32) -> i32, arg: i32) -> i32` and `fn add_one(x: i32) -> i32 { x + 1 }`, which statement about the function pointer type `fn` is true?

options.A: `fn` is a trait, so `do_twice` uses dynamic dispatch to call `f`
options.B: `fn(i32) -> i32` is a concrete type that implements all three closure traits (`Fn`, `FnMut`, `FnOnce`), so a function pointer can always be passed where a closure trait bound is expected -- but a capturing closure cannot be passed as `fn`
options.C: Closures and function pointers are the same type, so `do_twice` accepts any closure, including capturing ones
options.D: Function pointers only implement `FnOnce`, so `do_twice` could call `f` at most once

questions.explanation: `fn` (lowercase) is a concrete type, not a trait. Function pointers implement `Fn`, `FnMut`, and `FnOnce`, so APIs with generic closure bounds also accept plain functions (e.g. `map(ToString::to_string)`). The reverse is limited: only non-capturing closures coerce to `fn`. That is why accepting `impl Fn(i32) -> i32` is usually preferred unless you need `fn` for C interop.

questions.prompt: This function fails to compile. What is the problem?

options.A: The closure captures `a` from its environment, so it cannot coerce to a plain function pointer; return `impl Fn(i32) -> i32` (or `Box<dyn Fn(i32) -> i32>` when multiple call sites must share one type) instead
options.B: `move` is illegal in a return position; deleting `move` makes it compile
options.C: Closures can never be returned from functions in Rust
options.D: The return type must be spelled `Fn(i32) -> i32` with a capital F

questions.explanation: Each closure has its own anonymous type; only closures that capture nothing can coerce to a `fn` pointer, and this one captures `a`. Returning `impl Fn(i32) -> i32` works for one closure per function; if you need to return closures from several functions and store them together (e.g. in a `Vec`), each `impl Trait` is a distinct opaque type, so use `Box<dyn Fn(i32) -> i32>`. Bare `Fn(...)` is a trait and cannot be a return type by itself.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.C: Compile error: `$x` can only match a single expression, not a comma-separated list
options.D: `3`, because only the last match of `$x` is kept

questions.explanation: `$( $x:expr ),*` matches zero or more comma-separated expressions; here `$x` matches `1`, `2`, and `3` in turn. The `$( ... )*` in the body repeats `temp_vec.push($x);` once per match, so the call expands to a block that pushes 1, 2, 3 and evaluates to the vector. The sum is 6.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are real differences between macros and functions in Rust? Select all that apply.

options.A: A macro can accept a variable number of arguments (e.g. `println!` with one or many), while a function signature fixes the number and types of parameters
options.B: Macros are expanded before the compiler interprets the meaning of the code, so a macro can generate items such as a trait implementation on a given type; a function cannot, because it is called at runtime
options.C: Macros must be defined or brought into scope before they are called in a file
options.D: Functions can be defined anywhere and called anywhere in the same scope, without ordering constraints
options.E: Macro definitions are generally simpler to read and maintain than equivalent function definitions

questions.explanation: Macros are a form of metaprogramming: they expand into code at compile time, which lets them take variable argument counts and generate items like trait impls. Unlike functions, macros must be in scope before the call site in the file. The trade-off is the opposite of E: macro definitions are more complex and harder to read and maintain than functions.

questions.prompt: What are the three kinds of procedural macros in Rust?

options.A: Custom `derive` macros, attribute-like macros, and function-like macros
options.B: Declarative macros, hygienic macros, and recursive macros
options.C: Inline macros, module macros, and crate macros
options.D: `macro_rules!` macros, `#[derive]` macros, and inline assembly macros

questions.explanation: Procedural macros come in three kinds: custom derive (used as `#[derive(HelloMacro)]` on structs and enums), attribute-like (creating new attributes such as `#[route(GET, "/")]`, usable on more item kinds), and function-like (called like `sql!(SELECT * FROM posts)`). Declarative `macro_rules!` macros are a separate, non-procedural category.

questions.prompt: A crate defines a custom derive macro as shown. What happens when a user writes `#[derive(HelloMacro)] struct Pancakes;`?

options.A: At runtime, the first call to `Pancakes::hello_macro()` invokes `hello_macro_derive` reflectively to build the implementation
options.B: At compile time, the compiler passes the tokens of the `Pancakes` definition to `hello_macro_derive`; the function parses them (typically with `syn`), generates code (typically with `quote!`), and the returned `TokenStream` is added to the user's crate as the trait implementation
options.C: The compiler copies the source text of `impl_hello_macro` into the user's crate verbatim, without running any code
options.D: It fails unless `hello_macro_derive` is defined in the same crate as `struct Pancakes`, because of the orphan rule

questions.explanation: Procedural macros are functions from `TokenStream` to `TokenStream` that the compiler runs during compilation. For a derive macro, the input is the annotated item's tokens; `syn::parse` turns them into a `DeriveInput` syntax tree, and `quote!` builds the generated impl, which the compiler appends to the user's code. Derive macros must live in their own crate with `proc-macro = true` -- typically a separate crate from the user's code, so D is wrong, and nothing happens at runtime.
